intro: The seraphim (Hebrew seraphim, singular seraph, meaning "burning ones") are supernatural celestial beings described exclusively in Isaiah's throne-room vision (Isa. 6:1–7). Each possessed six wings: two to cover the face before God's glory, two to cover the feet in humility, and two for flight. They called to one another in antiphonal proclamation: "Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory" (Isa. 6:3), the threefold repetition (trisagion) underscoring the absolute holiness of God. One seraph flew to Isaiah with a burning coal from the heavenly altar and touched his lips, purging his guilt and atoning for his sin (Isa. 6:6–7), enabling the prophet's commission. The seraphim appear only here in the OT; their precise relationship to the cherubim, the living creatures of Ezekiel, and the four living creatures of Revelation 4 is debated by scholars.
